explicit_text: My nephew went to Korea to teach Kendo, and my mother went with him alone because he wasn't married yet. She stayed there for three years, teaching farmers in nearby villages how to make traditional items like mino and mushiro. While in Korea, she even helped improve their bean drying methods by introducing mushiro mats to keep sand and pebbles out of the azuki beans. It was around 60 years ago when she served those Korean villages, and her efforts were even featured in a newspaper article.
implicit_text: My nephew went abroad to teach Kendo, and my mother went with him alone because he wasn't married yet. She stayed there for three years, teaching farmers in nearby villages how to make traditional items like straw raincoats and drying mats. While there, she even helped improve their bean drying methods by introducing mats to keep sand and pebbles out of the small red beans. It was around 60 years ago when she served those villages, and her efforts were even featured in a newspaper article.

explicit_text: While my mother was in Korea, she taught the farmers how to make mino, which are traditional straw raincoats. This was part of her efforts to help the local villagers improve their daily lives. She was very skilled and able, and her teaching of mino was just one of several crafts she shared with the Korean communities during her three-year stay there. It was a meaningful way she contributed during that time.
implicit_text: While my mother was overseas, she taught the farmers how to make traditional straw raincoats. This was part of her efforts to help the local villagers improve their daily lives. She was very skilled and able, and her teaching of these raincoats was just one of several crafts she shared with the communities during her three-year stay there. It was a meaningful way she contributed during that time.

explicit_text: My mother taught the Korean farmers how to make mushiro, which are mats used for drying things like azuki beans. Before she introduced mushiro, the beans were dried on bridges and got mixed with pebbles and sand. After she showed them how to use mushiro mats, the quality of the beans improved because no more sand or pebbles got mixed in. This was one of the practical skills she shared during her time in Korea, and it was even written about in a newspaper.
implicit_text: My mother taught the farmers how to make mats used for drying things like small red beans. Before she introduced these mats, the beans were dried on bridges and got mixed with pebbles and sand. After she showed them how to use these mats, the quality of the beans improved because no more sand or pebbles got mixed in. This was one of the practical skills she shared during her time abroad, and it was even written about in a newspaper.

explicit_text: I remember bringing azuki beans that often had sand mixed in them because they were dried on bridges. My mother taught the Korean farmers how to use mushiro mats to dry the azuki beans properly, which stopped the sand and pebbles from mixing in. This practical lesson helped improve the quality of their beans significantly. It was part of her broader efforts to teach traditional crafts and improve farming practices in Korea.
implicit_text: I remember bringing small red beans that often had sand mixed in them because they were dried on bridges. My mother taught the farmers how to use mats to dry the beans properly, which stopped the sand and pebbles from mixing in. This practical lesson helped improve the quality of their beans significantly. It was part of her broader efforts to teach traditional crafts and improve farming practices there.

explicit_text: My mother also taught the making of goza, which are traditional woven mats, to the Korean villagers during her stay there. Along with mino, mushiro, and waraji, goza was one of the crafts she shared to help improve their livelihoods. She was very able and her work was recognized in a newspaper article about her service to the Korean villages. Teaching goza was part of her dedication to helping others through practical skills.
implicit_text: My mother also taught the making of traditional woven mats to the villagers during her stay there. explicit_text: During her time in Korea, my mother taught the villagers how to make waraji, traditional straw sandals. This was among several crafts like mino, mushiro, and goza that she taught to help improve their daily lives. Her ability to share these skills was remarkable and even got written up in a newspaper. Waraji making was an important part of her contribution to the community there.

explicit_text: My father used to drink sake a little every night and sometimes invited me to drink with him, just one sakazuki. That's how I learned to like sake and other drinks like wine and beer, but only a little at a time. It was a small but meaningful part of my family life growing up. My father never scolded me, and those moments with sake were gentle and bonding experiences.
implicit_text: My father used to drink a little rice wine every night and sometimes invited me to drink with him, just one small cup. That's how I learned to like rice wine and other drinks like wine and beer, but only a little at a time. It was a small but meaningful part of my family life growing up. My father never scolded me, and those moments with the rice wine were gentle and bonding experiences.

explicit_text: I was very good at soroban, the traditional abacus, and used to stand in front of the class calling out numbers for addition while calculating on my soroban. It was my best subject in school, and I can still do it faster than machines when I go to stores. My older and younger brothers were also good at it, and my younger brother still writes to me with very neat handwriting even now. Soroban was a skill I took pride in during my school days.
implicit_text: I was very good at the traditional abacus and used to stand in front of the class calling out numbers for addition while calculating on it. It was my best subject in school, and I can still do it faster than machines when I go to stores. My older and younger brothers were also good at it, and my younger brother still writes to me with very neat handwriting even now. This skill was something I took pride in during my school days.

explicit_text: After I got married, my husband and I left our hometown and went to Yokohama, staying in an inn there for nine days before coming to America. I didn't know at the time that my husband didn't have much money; he mostly stayed in the inn sleeping and pretending to be drunk on whisky. We left early because my mother thought I didn't have to go to America to get married, and we planned to stay only three years. Yokohama was a brief but important stop in our journey to America.
implicit_text: After I got married, my husband and I left our hometown and went to a port city near Tokyo, staying in an inn there for nine days before coming to America. I didn't know at the time that my husband didn't have much money; he mostly stayed in the inn sleeping and pretending to be drunk on whisky. We left early because my mother thought I didn't have to go to America to get married, and we planned to stay only three years. That city was a brief but important stop in our journey to America.

explicit_text: When we stayed in Yokohama before coming to America, my husband pretended to be drunk and sleepy from whisky, but actually he was just tired and didn't have enough money to take me out. I think it was whisky he pretended to drink, though I'm not completely sure. It was a difficult time for him, and I understood that he was struggling financially. Whisky was part of that memory of our early days before settling in America.
implicit_text: When we stayed in that port city before coming to America, my husband pretended to be drunk and sleepy from whisky, but actually he was just tired and didn't have enough money to take me out. I think it was whisky he pretended to drink, though I'm not completely sure. It was a difficult time for him, and I understood that he was struggling financially. That drink was part of that memory of our early days before settling in America.

explicit_text: When I was 18, I went to Kobe to learn how to make men's garments for nine months because I felt there was no future in our village business. My aunt lived there, and at first, I went without my parents' permission, so my mother came to take me home. Kobe was where I gained important skills that helped me work as a dressmaker later. It was a significant step in my life before marriage and moving abroad.
implicit_text: When I was 18, I went to a city known for its port and industry to learn how to make men's garments for nine months because I felt there was no future in our village business. My aunt lived there, and at first, I went without my parents' permission, so my mother came to take me home. That city was where I gained important skills that helped me work as a dressmaker later. It was a significant step in my life before marriage and moving abroad.

explicit_text: Although I didn't want to get married at first, I eventually married into the Akiyama family. The transcript doesn't mention a formal omiai, but marriage was a significant event in my life, especially since I was considered an old maid at 21. I had saved money from my dressmaking work and was independent before marriage. My marriage led us to move to Yokohama and eventually to America.
implicit_text: Although I didn't want to get married at first, I eventually married into a particular family. The transcript doesn't mention a formal arranged meeting, but marriage was a significant event in my life, especially since I was considered an old maid at 21. I had saved money from my dressmaking work and was independent before marriage. My marriage led us to move to a port city near Tokyo and eventually to America.

explicit_text: I married into the Akiyama family, and after marriage, we left our hometown and eventually came to America. When I returned to Japan after nine years, I contributed a lot to the Akiyama family, including building a large grave called Rekidai Bako to store bones of many generations. I spent about 20,000 yen on this and other community contributions in the family's name. Even though I lived in America, I wanted to honor and support the Akiyama family and our village.

I spent about 20,000 yen on this and other community contributions in the family's name. Even though I lived in America, I wanted to honor and support that family and our village.

explicit_text: When I was a small child, I was quite spoiled and tired easily, especially during visits to shrines with my mother. I would often ask her to hire a rickshaw so I wouldn't have to walk. I remember being carried on my older brother's back in bad weather, but the rickshaw was my favorite way to avoid walking. It was a small but memorable part of my childhood.
implicit_text: When I was a small child, I was quite spoiled and tired easily, especially during visits to shrines with my mother. I would often ask her to hire a pulled carriage so I wouldn't have to walk. I remember being carried on my older brother's back in bad weather, but the pulled carriage was my favorite way to avoid walking. It was a small but memorable part of my childhood.

explicit_text: Before I left my village to get married, I entrusted a man named Mr. Kakuichi Harada with the money I had saved from my dressmaking work, about 800 yen, asking him to keep it safe until I returned. When I came back after nine years, my father told me that Mr. Harada had revealed the secret because the family was struggling financially. I was grateful that the money helped my family during hard times, and I appreciated Mr. Harada's honesty and trustworthiness.
